Jesus sends the Twelve out with real authority—power over demons, power to heal—and then strips them of everything else. No staff, no bag, no money. No second tunic. They are sent into villages completely dependent on the hospitality of strangers and the provision of God. This is not gentle encouragement toward self-denial. This is Jesus asking them to trust Him with their survival. And they go. They preach the kingdom. They heal the sick. They return with stories of what God did through them. But notice what happens next: Jesus takes them away to be alone, and crowds find them anyway. He welcomes the crowds. He heals them. When His disciples suggest dismissing everyone so they can find food, Jesus turns the problem back on them: "You give them something to eat." With five loaves and two fish for five thousand people, Jesus does not perform a magic trick. He takes what they have, blesses it, breaks it, and gives it to His disciples to distribute. And there is more than enough. The lesson is not that Jesus can multiply food. The lesson is that when you surrender what you have to Him, He is faithful to provide.

Then comes the pivot point of Luke's entire gospel. Jesus asks His disciples who people say He is, and they report the popular theories—John the Baptist, Elijah, a prophet. Jesus presses them: "But what about you? Who do you say I am?" Peter answers correctly: "The Christ of God." And immediately, Jesus tells them to keep silent and explains what this actually means. The Christ must suffer. He must be rejected by the elders, the chief priests, the scribes. He must be killed. And on the third day, be raised. This is not the Messiah they expected. This is a Messiah who loses everything so that His people might be saved. And if you want to follow this Christ, you must deny yourself and take up your cross daily. Not as metaphor. As reality. To follow Jesus is to walk the same path He walks—a path of self-denial, suffering, and death to your own will. Jesus does not soften this. He does not offer an easier version of discipleship for those who cannot handle the hard one. This is what it means to follow Him.

Then, eight days later, Jesus takes Peter, John, and James up a mountain to pray, and His face changes. His clothes become radiantly white. Moses and Elijah appear with Him, speaking about His "departure"—the exodus He is about to accomplish at Jerusalem. It is a glimpse of Christ's glory, of who He truly is, and it is inseparable from what He is about to suffer and accomplish through His death. Peter, half-awake and terrified, suggests building three shelters, as if to preserve the moment. But a cloud covers them. The voice of God the Father speaks from the cloud: "This is My Son, whom I have chosen. Listen to Him." Not honor Him. Not believe in Him, though that is true. Listen to Him. Hear what He is telling you about suffering, about denial, about losing your life to save it. When the cloud clears, Jesus stands alone. The vision is over. And immediately, when they come down the mountain, a father begs Jesus to heal his son, seized and convulsed by a demon. Jesus does it. He rebukes the unclean spirit. The boy is healed. The crowds are astonished. And in the middle of their amazement, Jesus leans down to His disciples and says: "Let these words sink into your ears: The Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men." They do not understand. They cannot comprehend it. They are afraid to ask. Instead, they argue about which of them is the greatest.

Jesus reads their hearts. He sets a little child beside Him and tells them that whoever welcomes the least among them welcomes Him, and whoever welcomes Him welcomes God the Father. Greatness in the kingdom of God is measured by how you treat the powerless. Then John reports that they saw someone driving out demons in Jesus' name, someone not part of their group, and they tried to stop him. Jesus tells them not to. "Whoever is not against you is for you." You are not gatekeepers of My power. You are not arbiters of who belongs in My kingdom. Your job is to listen to Me, to take up your cross, to lose your life for My sake. Everything else—the arguments about status, the jealous guarding of authority, the fear about what happens next—these are the chains you must break. Luke shows us a chapter of extraordinary power: demons fleeing, the sick healed, five thousand fed, the glory of Christ revealed on a mountain
